stem: Recognize the following limit as a definite integral over \( [0, 1] \) and evaluate it: \( \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ ([[a]])\left(\frac{i}{n}\right)^{2} + ([[b]])\left(\frac{i}{n}\right) + ([[c]]) \right] \).
solution: With \( x_i = i/n \) and \( \Delta x = 1/n \), the sum is a right Riemann sum for \( \int_{0}^{1} \left( ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \right) dx \). Evaluating gives \( \frac{[[a]]}{3} + \frac{[[b]]}{2} + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the integrand at \( x = 1 \) instead of integrating over \( [0, 1] \).
This integrates the \( x^{2} \) term as if it were linear (dividing by 2 instead of 3).
This drops the constant term, which integrates to itself over an interval of length 1.
This mishandles the linear term, dividing by 3 rather than 2.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Recognize the following limit as a definite integral over \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \) and evaluate it: \( \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ ([[m]])\left([[a]] + \frac{i([[b]]-[[a]])}{n}\right) + ([[q]]) \right] \cdot \frac{[[b]]-[[a]]}{n} \).
solution: With sample points \( x_i = [[a]] + i([[b]]-[[a]])/n \) and \( \Delta x = ([[b]]-[[a]])/n \), this is a right Riemann sum for \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left( ([[m]])x + ([[q]]) \right) dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the integrand as constant at its LEFT-endpoint value \( f([[a]]) \) times the interval width, instead of integrating.
This treats the integrand as constant at its RIGHT-endpoint value \( f([[b]]) \) times the interval width, instead of integrating.
This forgets the factor of \( [[b]]-[[a]] \) on the linear term while keeping it on the constant term.
This drops the factor of \( \tfrac12 \) from the power rule, using \( b^2-a^2 \) directly instead of \( \tfrac12(b^2-a^2) \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Recognize the following as a LEFT-endpoint Riemann sum converging to a definite integral over \( [0, 1] \), and evaluate it: \( \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \left[ ([[a]])\left(\frac{i}{n}\right)^{2} + ([[b]])\left(\frac{i}{n}\right) + ([[c]]) \right] \).
solution: Even though the sample points are \( x_i = i/n \) for \( i=0,\dots,n-1 \) (the LEFT endpoints of each subinterval), the limit converges to the SAME definite integral as any other choice of sample points: \( \int_{0}^{1} \left( ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \right) dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the integrand only at \( x=0 \) (the very first left endpoint), instead of integrating over the whole interval.
This evaluates the integrand at \( x=1 \) as if it were a right-endpoint sum, ignoring that this is a left-endpoint sum with the same limiting integral.
This doubles the constant term's contribution, as if left- and right-endpoint sums gave different limiting integrals.
This wrongly assumes a left-endpoint sum must vanish in the limit.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Recognize the following limit as a definite integral over \( [0, 1] \) and evaluate it: \( \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{[[c]]}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{\frac{i}{n}} \).
solution: With \( x_i=i/n \) and \( \Delta x=1/n \), the sum is a right Riemann sum for \( \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{x}\, dx = \tfrac{2}{3} \). Scaled by \( [[c]] \), the limit is \( [[c]]\cdot\tfrac{2}{3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats \( \sqrt{x} \) as if it integrated to 1 over \( [0,1] \), forgetting the power-rule factor.
This uses \( \tfrac12 \), the exponent-based guess, instead of \( \int_0^1 \sqrt{x}\,dx=\tfrac23 \).
This inverts the fraction \( \tfrac23 \) into \( \tfrac32 \).
This applies the power rule as if the exponent of \( \sqrt{x} \) were \( 2 \) instead of \( \tfrac12 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: Recognize the following limit as a definite integral over \( [0, \pi/2] \) and evaluate it: \( \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{[[p]]\pi}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sin\!\left(\frac{i\pi}{2n}\right) \).
solution: With \( x_i = i\pi/(2n) \) and \( \Delta x=\pi/(2n) \), the sum is a right Riemann sum for \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \sin(x)\,dx = 1 \). Scaled by \( [[p]] \), the limit is \( [[p]]\cdot 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the length of the interval \( \pi/2 \) scaled by \( [[p]] \) instead of the value of the integral.
This wrongly assumes the integral of sine over this interval is 0, as it would be over a full period.
This drops the correct sign, as if \( \int_0^{\pi/2}\sin(x)\,dx=-1 \).
This halves the coefficient \( [[p]] \) for no valid reason.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: Recognize the following MIDPOINT Riemann sum as converging to a definite integral over \( [0, 1] \), and evaluate it: \( \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ ([[a]])\left(\frac{i - 1/2}{n}\right)^{2} + ([[b]])\left(\frac{i-1/2}{n}\right) + ([[c]]) \right] \).
solution: The sample points \( x_i = (i-\tfrac12)/n \) are the MIDPOINTS of each subinterval of \( [0,1] \); like left- or right-endpoint sums, a midpoint sum converges to the same definite integral: \( \int_{0}^{1} \left( ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \right) dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This plugs the midpoint of the WHOLE interval, \( x=1/2 \), into the integrand as if that alone gave the integral.
This evaluates the integrand at \( x=1 \), treating it as a right-endpoint sum instead of a midpoint sum with the same limiting integral.
This doubles the constant term's contribution, as if a midpoint sum gave a different limiting integral than the true one.
This keeps only the constant term, dropping the quadratic and linear contributions.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
